coredata.dc:title: High strength-tough and super-dispersed carbonized polymer dots reinforced copper composites fabricated by ultrasonic spray pyrolysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhaojie
Liu, Liang
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Li, Caiju
Liu, Yichun
Kong, Xin
Tan, Songlin
Xu, Zunyan
coredata.dc:description: Dense intragranular distribution of nanoscale reinforcements is highly desirable since the effectiveness in balancing the strength-ductility trade-off in metal matrix composites. Herein, the extremely fine-sized carbonized polymer dots (CPDs, ∼4.3 nm) was selected as a novel ex-situ reinforcement, ultrasonic spray pyrolysis was employed to maximize the elimination of CPDs agglomeration and achieved the uniform intragranular distribution. Micro-pillar compression was conducted to uncover the intrinsic relationship between interface features and mechanical performance. Results revealed that the CPDs/Cu composite exhibited significant work hardening and strain delocalization, which were attributed to the exceptional strain/shear accommodation capability of the amorphous interface layers (AIL). Furthermore, the formation and transmission of shear transformation zones (STZs) would blunt the stress concentration associated with the dislocation plugging and in turn homogenize the plastic flow of composites. Strikingly, CPDs/Cu composites possessed extraordinary comprehensive properties, demonstrated the ultimate tensile strength, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity of 470 MPa, 390 W/m·K and 93.2 % IACS, respectively. This work underscores the importance of nano-particle spatial distribution and interface structure configurations in regulating Cu composite properties for structural-functional applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs)
Metal matrix composites
Spraying pyrolysis
Mechanical performance
Interface characteristics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Micro-pillar compression test of different types CPDs-Cu composites
Macroscopic mechanical, thermal and electrical properties
Length-scale effects on the spatial distribution of CPDs particles
Dislocation interaction behavior of different types of CPDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the processing route of CPDs/Cu composites; (b, c) Pilot scale production spray pyrolysis plant for preparation of CPDs-CuxO powders; (d); Batch-produced CPDs-CuxO powders.

(a) TEM characterizations and particle size statistics of CPDs; (b) XPS C1s spectra for CPDs; (c) CPDs solution emits blue fluorescence under UV irradiation; (d) Formation mechanism of core-shell CPDs-CuxO powders prepared by USP; (e, f) TEM images of CPDs-CuxO powders and corresponding elemental mappings (g).

EBSD characterization of pure Cu and CPDs/Cu composites: (a, b) Orientation imaging maps; (a1, b1) KAM distribution maps; (c) Grain size statistics; (d) KAM distribution and (e) Schmid factor distribution for pure Cu and CPDs/Cu composites.

Representative TEM images and interface structure schematic of composites containing different types of CPDs: (a, b) TEM images of CPDs/Cu composites; (c) Percentage of intragranular and intergranular CPDs and their particle size distribution in composites; (d) High-angle annular dark-field-STEM image and corresponding EDS mappings of the selected region in (b); (e, f) HRTEM images taken at the CPDs/Cu interface; (g) HRTEM image taken at the HT-CPDs/Cu interface; (h) Schematic diagram of the interface structure of AIL and CIL, respectively.

Compressive stress-strain curves and SEM images of HT-CPDs/Cu and CPDs/Cu micro-pillars. (a) The schematic of micro-pillar processing; Compressive stress-strain curves of (b) HT-CPDs/Cu and (c) CPDs/Cu micro-pillar (shear mode Ⅰ: shear occurs immediately after yielding, shear mode Ⅱ: a short strain hardening stage occurs precedes unstable shearing event); SEM images of post-compressed of (b1, b2) HT-CPDs/Cu micro-pillars and (c1, c2) CPDs/Cu micro-pillars.

Representative TEM images of HT-CPDs/Cu and CPDs/Cu composites micro-pillars after compression: (a) TEM image of whole HT-CPDs/Cu pillars; (b, c) HRTEM images of chosen regions marked in (a); (d) TEM image of whole CPDs/Cu pillars; (e, f) HRTEM images of chosen regions marked in (d).

Tensile properties, electrical conductivity and thermal conductivity of pure Cu and composites: (a) Engineering stress-strain curves, (b) True stress-strain curves and (c) Strain hardening rate curves of pure Cu, HT-CPDs/Cu and CPDs/Cu composites; (d) Radar chart of mechanical, electrical and thermal properties comparisons; (e, f) Tensile strength versus electrical conductivity and thermal conductivity of CPDs/Cu composites as comparison with reported Cu-based composites.

Schematic diagram of GB migration during consolidation and thermal deformation, where smaller sized intergranular CPDs particles more readily separate from grain boundaries and ultimately transform into intragranular.

Schematic diagram of dislocation-interface interactions for CPDs containing CIL (a) and AIL (b), respectively. (a1-a3) Dislocation transfer is impeded by CIL leading to stress concentration and produce cracking defects. (b1-b3) Dislocations are absorbed and accommodated by the AIL and converted to STZs, which are transmitted in the AIL to achieve dislocations cross-interface transmit.
Tensile properties, electrical and thermal conductivity of Cu Composites and pure Cu.

head.ce:title: High strength-tough and super-dispersed carbonized polymer dots reinforced copper composites fabricated by ultrasonic spray pyrolysis

Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dense intragranular distribution of nanoscale reinforcements is highly desirable since the effectiveness in balancing the strength-ductility trade-off in metal matrix composites. Herein, the extremely fine-sized carbonized polymer dots (CPDs, ∼4.3 nm) was selected as a novel ex-situ reinforcement, ultrasonic spray pyrolysis was employed to maximize the elimination of CPDs agglomeration and achieved the uniform intragranular distribution. Micro-pillar compression was conducted to uncover the intrinsic relationship between interface features and mechanical performance. Results revealed that the CPDs/Cu composite exhibited significant work hardening and strain delocalization, which were attributed to the exceptional strain/shear accommodation capability of the amorphous interface layers (AIL). Furthermore, the formation and transmission of shear transformation zones (STZs) would blunt the stress concentration associated with the dislocation plugging and in turn homogenize the plastic flow of composites. Strikingly, CPDs/Cu composites possessed extraordinary comprehensive properties, demonstrated the ultimate tensile strength, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity of 470 MPa, 390 W/m·K and 93.2 % IACS, respectively. This work underscores the importance of nano-particle spatial distribution and interface structure configurations in regulating Cu composite properties for structural-functional applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Interface, as a “bond” connecting matrix and reinforcement, is the core of enhancement effect and the key to improving the composite performances [1–3]. With advancements in nanotechnology, the size of reinforcement particles has shifted from microscopic dimensions to the nanoscale, resulting in a substantial increase in the volume fraction of the interface [4–6]. For instance, when particle size decreases from 1 μm to 10 nm, the interface concentration would increase by 106 times. Therefore, interface characteristics become increasingly crucial in determining the composite comprehensive performance as the reinforcement size decreases.

Despite advancements in the field of nanocomposites, several unsettled challenges remain to be addressed. Firstly, in order to maximize the reinforcement benefits, it is imperative to mitigate or eradicate nanoparticle agglomeration and ensure a mono-disperse of particle within the matrix. Otherwise, resulting in severe stress concentration or strain localization [4,7,8]. From the perspective of reinforcement distribution position, nanoscale particles can be sufficiently small to package in the grain interior [9–11]. This configuration is advantageous for enhancing mechanical properties via Orowan mechanism while alleviating the stress concentration arising from excessive dislocation plugging at GB [12,13]. However, preventing nanoparticle aggregation is difficult owing to their high specific surface energy, and not to mention regulate intragranular mono-disperse [14,15]. Moreover, there is a dearth of basic research on the mechanisms governing nanoparticle intragranular distribution, and the relevant guidance model remains to be established.

On the other hand, the interface not only serves as the primary channel for transmitting external loads, but also plays a crucial role in influencing the deformation mechanism of composite [16–18]. Some seminal studies demonstrated the significant impact of the microstructure and chemistry of interface characteristics on composite mechanical properties [19,20]. Literature abounds with examples of materials containing serrated interfaces, chemically mixed interfaces, or three-dimensional interfaces et al. For example, Guo et al. [21] constructed a continuous serrated graphene interface, which improved the critical resolved shear stress for dislocation initiation and increased dislocation storage capacity near the interface area. They posited that this special interface configuration was crucial for intrinsic toughening and regulating the plastic deformation process. Zhang et al. [2] found that the formation of a mixed transitional interface layer between RGO and Cu led to significant alterations in the interaction and multiplication of dislocations. Shi et al. [22] discovered that the typical amorphous interface layers (AIL) in Cu/Nb nano-laminated was able to coordinate the interface deformation by forming shear transformation zones (STZs) to mitigate shear localization. Therefore, a comprehensive investigation into the nature and role of the interface, especially for the interface deformation behavior is essential. Until now, studies have mainly concentrated on the fabrication process and mechanical characteristics of nano-composites. However, exploring the impact of the interface on defect formation, as well as the complex dislocation behavior near the interface region is still an open and controversial topic.

Promisingly, in our previous reports, carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) reinforced Cu matrix composites have been successfully fabricated using powder metallurgy method [23,24]. These novel CPDs/Cu composites enabled the simultaneous enhancement of several mutually exclusive properties, such as strength-electrical conductivity, strength-thermal stability, and strength-ductility. These exceptional comprehensive properties are attributed to the distinctive quantum size effect of CPDs and its special intragranular distribution configuration. Furthermore, the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups and C–C sp3 dangling bond on the CPDs surface would promote the formation of amorphous interface layers and facilitate the interface bonding [25]. These findings had suggested the prospect of CPDs in engineering develop high-performance nanostructured materials. Therefore, a comprehensive understanding of the CPDs size effect on its spatial distribution patterns within Cu grains is non-negligible. Moreover, the impact of the amorphous interface transitional layer on the CPDs/Cu composites deformation and failure behavior still needs to be further explored.

In view of the above issues, employing CPDs-Cu as the model materials, we have developed ultrasonic spray pyrolysis (USP) for achieving nanoparticles homo-disperse. Two types of reinforcements, known as high surface-active CPDs and low surface-active CPDs (experienced the heat treatment carbonization, labeled as HT-CPDs) were used to fabricate the CPDs/Cu and HT-CPDs/Cu bulk composites, respectively. High surface-active CPDs can interact with Cu matrix and form a typical amorphous interface layer (AIL). In comparison, HT-CPDs were utilized to prepare composites without an AIL transition area. Micro-pillar compression tests were conducted to investigate the dislocation behavior and deformation accommodation capability for different interface types. In addition, the spatial distribution of CPDs particles/clusters and its corresponding intragranular distribution mechanisms were systemically investigated. Moreover, a detailed analysis was provided on the exceptional coordinated deformation capability of AIL in achieving good shear stability and ductility of CPDs/Cu composites.

Firstly, CPDs was synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method [26]. In brief, 15 mL ethylenediamine and 60.0 g citric acid were dissolved in 900 mL deionized water. Then, the solution was added to an autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 6 h. The synthesized solution was subjected to dialysis for 60 h to remove unreacted precursors. Finally, CPDs powder was prepared by freeze-drying method. The CPDs-CuxO composite powders were then synthesized via ultrasonic spraying pyrolysis to disperse CPDs. Typically, Cu(CH3COO)2·H2O (CuAc) and CPDs were dissolved in deionized water at a fixed molar ratio and stirred for 2 h to form a mixed CPDs-CuAc precursor solution. Subsequently, the CPDs-CuAc solution was transferred to an atomization unit equipped with a 32 × 2.4-MHz ultrasonic spray generator to generate droplets at a rate of 20 L/h. These droplets were then conveyed to a thermal reaction zone with a consistent flow rate of 0.35 L/min, where droplets were thermally decomposed at 750 °C and produced CPDs-CuxO composite powders. At present, the engineering preparation of CPDs-CuxO composite powders has been achieved by the pilot scale production spray pyrolysis plant independently developed in the laboratory (Fig. 1 (b and c)). The batch produced CPDs-CuxO composite powders is shown in Fig. 1 (d).

The schematic diagram in Fig. 1(a) illustrates the preparation process of CPDs/Cu composites. Firstly, the collected CPDs-CuxO powders were subjected to reduction at 350 °C for 3 h under H2 to obtain CPDs-Cu powders. The reduced CPDs-Cu powders were mixed with flaky Cu powders by means of shift speed ball-milling (200 rpm-8 h and 400 rpm-4 h) The obtained composite powders were then packed into carbide die (WC, ∼26 mm inner diameter) and consolidated via SPS at 575 °C for 10 min with an applied pressure of 400 MPa. Following sintering, the resulting cylinder-shaped bulks, measuring 12 mm in height and 26 mm in diameter, underwent hot rolling at 650 °C to a height reduction of 80 %. To reveal the impact of interfacial microscopic configuration on the composites’ mechanical behavior. For comparison, the other typed CPDs were preliminary heat-treated at 1650 °C for 4h to fully carbonize and remove the oxygen-contained functional group concurrently, so as to avoid the formation of amorphous interfacial layers (AIL). Hereafter, Cu bulk composites reinforced with 0.3 vol% original CPDs and 0.3 vol% heat-treated CPDs (HT-CPDs) were denoted as CPDs/Cu and HT-CPDs/Cu, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 6700) was utilized for the identification of functional groups present in CPDs. A high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Thermo F200s) was employed to recognize the microstructures of CPDs-CuxO powders and relevant bulk composites. The grain size and Schmid factor were characterized via electron backscattered diffraction (EBSD) technique integrated into the scanning electron microscopy (Nova Nano SEM 430). Uniaxial micro-pillar compression tests were performed to simultaneously understand the micro-interaction between the reinforcement and the matrix during deformation. Focused ion beam (FIB, Helios 600i) was employed to prepare micro-pillars with a height of 5–6 μm and a diameter of 2 μm from the surface of the bulk composite. Uniaxial micro-pillar compression tests were conducted in an ex-situ nanoindentation system (Hysitron TI 950), with a flat diamond punch of 5 μm. The compression tests were performed at room temperature with a constant strain rate of 1 × 10−3/s. The morphology of the post-deformed micro-pillar was recognized by SEM. The interface microstructures of the sheared/deformed micro-pillar were characterized by TEM, in which TEM samples were obtained by the lift-out method using FIB. In addition, uniaxial tensile properties were assessed by a universal testing machine (CMT6103), with a cross-head strain rate of 5 × 10−3/s, and three tensile tests were conducted on each group of composite samples. The thermal diffusivity was measured by a laser flash apparatus (LFA 467) at room temperature. Lastly, electrical conductivity (EC) was measured by a metal four probe tester (RTS-11) at room temperature. The electrical conductivity values are reported in terms of %IACS (International Annealed Copper Standard). The electrical conductivity of standard annealed copper at 20 °C is defined as 100 %IACS, with the specific value of 5.8 × 107 S per meter (S/m).

As depicted in Fig. 2 (a), the synthesized quasi-spherical CPDs nanoparticles exhibit a high crystallinity, with an average particle size of 4.3 nm. There are considerable amounts of hydrophilic groups on the CPDs surface ((Fig. 2(b)), which contributes to ensuring excellent water solubility and displays fluorescence properties under UV irradiation [27,28] ((Fig. 2(c)).

The excellent dispersibility of CPDs in solution combined with fast solvent evaporation in spray pyrolysis will contribute to achieving or maintaining dispersion. During the USP process (Fig. 2(d)), the atomized CPDs-CuAc droplets (typically average size is ∼25 μm) were transported by streams of N2 and passed through a thermal reaction region. Due to the high solubility of CuAc, as solvent evaporation ensued, the equilibrium concentration of CuAc leading to surface precipitation was achieved concurrently with the internal precipitation of the droplets. Consequently, the interior and surface of droplet simultaneously precipitate with homogeneous CPDs as the center until the CPDs are uniformly covered by the CuAc coating layers to form dense CPDs-CuAc particles. Subsequently, the refined CuAc coating layers underwent full thermal decomposition, leading to the complete transformation into copper oxide (CuxO) nanoparticles. Notably, the CPDs was “frozen” inside the CuxO nanoparticles and maintained its uniformly dispersed state, which was attributed to the “flash-evaporation” process [29]. This rapid pyrolysis could effectively inhibit CPDs agglomeration, and the CPDs would be firmly encapsulated in the in-situ formed CuxO. As illustrated in Fig. 2(e), the synthesized CPDs/CuxO composite powders exhibit a distinctive spherical core-shell architecture, with an average size of ∼119.4 nm. The enlarged image in Fig. 2(f) displays that the mono-dispersed CPDs were distributed inside the CuxO particles. The typical microscopic configuration resembles that of a mature pomegranate, where the grains (CPDs) are covered with a homogeneous pericarp (CuxO coating layers). TEM-EDX analysis (Fig. 2(g)) demonstrates a high concentration of C, O, and Cu in CPDs/CuxO particles, which further confirms that CPDs was encapsulated inside CuxO. The typical “pomegranate seed” configuration minimizes the propensity for CPDs agglomeration and facilitates the intragranular distribution of CPDs during the subsequent sintering process.

EBSD results of hot-rolled CPDs/Cu composites and pure Cu matrix are illustrated in Fig. 3 . They have retained the lamellated feature that was inherited from the milled flaky powders (Fig. 3(a and b)). Moreover, the average grain size of CPDs/Cu composites is ∼2.6 μm (Fig. 3(c)), being marginally smaller than that of pure Cu matrix (3.7 μm). It indicates that CPDs particles has a limited grain refinement effect. Such an anomaly can be explained by the fact that a small amount of CPDs introduction cannot effectively prevent the migration of grain boundaries (GB) through the Zener pinning effect. The Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) distribution results of CPDs/Cu composites demonstrate that non-uniform plastic deformation (appreciable stress concentration) is absent and there is almost no variation in defect density as compared to pure Cu (Fig. 3(a1, b1, d)). The average Schmid factor of pure Cu and CPDs/Cu are 0.464 and 0.461 (Fig. 3(e)), respectively, indicating no obvious crystallographic texture and preferential orientation formed in the hot rolled composites [30].

The typical microstructures of CPDs/Cu composites shown in Fig. 4 indicate that CPDs clusters/particles are finely dispersed, with CPDs predominantly located within the grains (Fig. 4(a and b)). Fig. 4(c) displays the percentage statistics of CPDs clusters/particles associated with different diameters located in GBs or grain interiors. The statistical histogram reveals that the average particle size of CPDs is about 56.4 ± 5 nm, with 80 % smaller CPDs clusters situated within grain interiors. For CPDs clusters/particles with diameters larger than ∼60 nm, they are usually distributed at the GB. In comparison, those smaller than ∼60 nm are predominantly intragranular dispersion. Therefore, the distribution pattern of CPDs particles is intricately associated with its size effect, with the critical size value of 60 nm serving as a pivotal parameter influencing the distribution state. More detailed analyses will be presented in the following Discussion section.

To further investigate the interface bonding state of CPDs-Cu, high-resolution TEM characterization was performed. The carbon element was found to be concentrated in the central region of CPDs particles, up to 94.5 wt%, as evidenced by dark-field-STEM and EDS mappings (Fig. 4(d)). Interestingly, the transition layers with contrast difference surrounding Cu matrix can be observed at the interface region. To better reveal the microstructure of the transition layer, atomic-scale investigations of the interfaces were carried out using high-resolution STEM. The transition layer (∼5 nm) is caught in the middle of Cu and CPDs (Fig. 4(e)). It was proved to be typical amorphous interface layers (AIL) [31,32] by HRTEM and Fourier Transform pattern (FFT) (Fig. 4(f)). The corresponding EDS analyses indicate that AIL primarily consists of Cu, C and O elements (Fig. S1). It is postulated that transition layers are more likely to be a Cu–O–C multi-element “intermixing region”. The formation of AIL is related to the presence of defects and oxygen-containing functional groups on the CPDs surface. During the SPS process, the activated O and suspended C atoms gaining energy from Joule would heat escape from CPDs, and diffuse to participate in the formation of Cu–O–C intermixing region, which ultimately forms AIL at the interface [2]. That is to say, the formation of AIL in solid-state is considered to be not only a thermally activated but also a diffusion-controlled process [33]. On the contrary, when the CPDs reinforcement had experienced HT process and removed the supererogatory oxygen-containing functional groups, the interface structure between HT-CPDs and Cu was free of typical AIL. The representative interface microstructure of HT-CPDs/Cu is shown in Fig. 4(g), CPDs and Cu have typical non-chemical bonding and form seamless crystalline interface layers (CIL). To reveal explicitly the influence of interface microstructure types (type I: AIL or type II: CIL, Fig. 4(h)) on the deformation behaviors, micro-pillar compression and macro mechanical tensile testing were conducted to investigate the inherent connection between interface features and performances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Micro-pillar compression test of different types CPDs-Cu composites
ce:section.ce:para: To intuitively reveal the interactions between dislocations and different typed interfaces and to analyze the corresponding interface deformation behaviors, compression tests were conducted on CPDs/Cu and HT-CPDs composites using uniaxial micro-pillar testing. These micro-pillars were fabricated in the manner depicted in Fig. 5 (a), wherein the micro-pillars are perpendicular to the laminates, resulting in an iso-strain configuration [34]. The representative compressive stress-strain curves and SEM images of post-compressed HT-CPDs/Cu and CPDs/Cu micro-pillars are shown in Fig. 5(b and c). Obviously, two types of deformation modes exist here: early shear occurs immediately after yielding (Fig. 5(b), shear mode Ⅰ) and short stable flow regime occurs prior to the unstable shearing event (Fig. 5(c), shear mode Ⅱ). The micro-pillars of HT-CPDs/Cu composites displayed lower yield strength (200 ± 10.4 MPa) and compressive strain (14.7 %), as depicted in Fig. 5(b). Combined with SEM characterization of the post-deformed micro-pillars, it indicates that an initial shear occurs along the slip surface after the yielding. With increasing external loads, a predominant and localized shear band initiates at the top of pillars and propagates laterally, ultimately resulting in complete shearing and a strain excursion of approximately 5 % (Fig. 5(b1, b2)). The deformation of HT-CPDs/Cu micro-pillars is concentrated in a specific region, demonstrating pronounced shear deformation and geometric instability.

On the contrary, CPDs/Cu composite pillars display exceptional yield strength (445.6 ± 8.8 MPa) and outstanding elongation (28.3 %), as illustrated in Fig. 5(c). Furthermore, CPDs/Cu pillars demonstrate significant strain hardening during early compression and prolonged strain excursion when the pillar reached its strength limits. Such observations are consistent with the SEM images of micro-pillars after compression (Fig. 5(c1, c2)). With increasing external load, a progressive shift occurs from lateral-bugle governed deformation (which corresponds to strain hardening) to a deformation pattern dominated by interface shearing/strain localization (which corresponds to strain excursion stage). The lateral surfaces of micro-pillars appear to be uniformly deformed over the entire height range, whereas no disastrous shear band was observed. Therefore, compared with HT-CPDs/Cu pillars, the overall uniform plastic deformation of CPDs/Cu micro-pillars is convinced evidence that the stable plastic flow is a result of the superior coordinated deformation, strain stiffening and strain accommodation capabilities of AIL.

To further reveal the failure mechanisms of different typed micro-pillars, TEM characterization was performed on the post-compressed samples. HT-CPDs/Cu micro-pillars exhibited complete shearing (physical separation) along the radial direction, accompanied by the formation of cracks and voids. (Fig. 6 (a and b)). It is noteworthy that a high concentration of dislocations was observed within the deformed grains, with a tendency to aggregate and accumulate at the CIL. Excessive plugging of dislocations at the CIL could result in stress concentration, leading to the formation of cracking defects that impede further strain hardening and ultimately contribute to the premature shear failure of micro-pillars. On the contrary, for CPDs/Cu micro-pillars (Fig. 6(d)), there are some evident slip lines distributed throughout the view field, without experiencing catastrophic shear deformation or excessive dislocation plugging. High magnified TEM pattern reveals that the moved dislocation lines would terminate at the CPDs-Cu interface and be absorbed by the AIL (Fig. 6(f)). This observation indicates that the AIL demonstrates excellent dislocation absorption and accommodation capabilities, which can disperse and modulate the dislocation plugging, and effectively remove the stress localization. Meanwhile, AIL can also serve as the primary site for dislocation initiation and maintain the prolonged dynamic equilibrium between dislocation annihilation and multiplication by continuously generating new dislocations. This contributes to achieving sustained strain hardening and obtaining higher tensile strength and uniform elongation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Macroscopic mechanical, thermal and electrical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The engineering tensile stress-strain curves of pure Cu, CPDs/Cu and HT-CPDs/Cu composites are presented in Fig. 7 (a). Strikingly, CPDs/Cu composites exhibit an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 470 ± 8 MPa and a yield strength (YS) of 371 ± 6 MPa, which are significantly higher than that of pure Cu. Remarkably, the incorporation of CPDs results in a significant increase in uniform elongation (UE) from 10.2 ± 0.4 % to 15.7 ± 0.3 %. Simultaneously, UTS and UE are also higher than HT-CPDs/Cu counterpart, increasing by 118.2 MPa and 7.8 %, respectively.

The true stress-strain curves and strain hardening rate curves of corresponding samples are shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c). Obviously, CPDs/Cu composites possess higher strain hardening rates with longer work hardening stages [21]. The favorable strength-ductility compatibility is ascribed to the dual role of the intragranular CPDs in blocking dislocations and storing dislocations, which avoids stress concentrations at GB and improves the work-hardening ability [9]. Comparations including thermal conductivity (TC), electrical conductivity (EC) and mechanical properties were summarized in a schematic radar chart (Fig. 7(d)), with detailed statistics provided in Table 1 . It is apparent that the TC and EC values of CPDs/Cu composites at room temperature (RT) are 390 W/m·K and 93.2 % IACS, respectively, slightly lower than those of the pure Cu matrix (410 W/m·K and 98.1 %IACS). Furthermore, it is demonstrated in Fig. 7(e, f) that CPDs/Cu composites exhibit a noteworthy combination of tensile strength, electrical conductivity [35–41] and thermal conductivity [42–46] when compared to previously reported Cu-based materials. Thus, these performance results emphasized that the intragranular CPDs distribution has negligible effect on the intrinsic electron/phonon migration behaviors, but gains significantly mechanical improvement. Obviously, CPDs/Cu composites demonstrate a favorable balance of high strength, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity, offering novel insights into the design of advanced structural-functional integrated composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Length-scale effects on the spatial distribution of CPDs particles
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that with the decrease of Cu grain size, achieving intragranular distribution of particulates is extremely challenging. Interestingly, our results indicated that ∼80 % of CPDs nanoparticles were located in grain interior, with the critical size determining the distribution modes estimated to be ∼60 nm. The mechanisms that dominate this distribution behavior are discussed as follows.

Firstly, when the driving force for GB migration (
Δ F
) is enough to overcome the Zener pinning force (
f Z
), the GB will disengage from particles, leaving the particles within the grains. The Zener pinning force (
f Z
) is significantly affected by particle size as can be assessed by Ref. [49]:
(1)
f Z
= 2 π r ∗ cos
θ ∗ sin
θ ∗ γ
Where r is particle size, θ is the angle between the direction of GB motion and the GB, and γ is the GB energy [50]. Consequently, compared with large-sized CPDs particles, smaller-sized CPDs markedly reduce the
f Z
, which thermo-dynamically facilitates the distribution within the grain (Fig. 9(a1)).

Second, particle size remarkably affects the mobility kinetics of GB and particles, driving the separation of particle from GB. The mobility of the near-spherical CPDs particles (
m p
) can be stated as follows [51]:
(2)
m p
=
Ω 2
ρ
S
d s
T k
π r
4
Where Ω is the atomic volume,
ρ S
is the surface density of atoms,
d s
is the surface diffusion constant,
k
is the Boltzmann's constant,
T
is the absolute temperature and
r
is the particle size. As shown in Eq. (2), the mobility of particles (
m p
) depends greatly on particle size, large size will significantly reduce the particles mobility kinetics. Importantly, the
m p
has a substantial influence on the GB mobility (
m b
), where
m b
is decided either by particle-free grain boundaries (
m
f b
) at high particle mobility or by GB particle mobility (
m p
n
,
n
is the number of particles per unit grain boundary region) at low particle mobility [6]. According to Gottstein's theory [51], the migration velocity of GB (
v
) can be expressed as follows:
(3)
v =
{
m
f b
∙ Δ F , r ≤
r c
Δ F ∙
m p
∫
r c
∞
n ‾
( r )
d r
=
Δ F
Ω 2
ρ
S
d s
π
r 4
k T
∫
r c
∞
n ‾
( r )
d r
, r >
r c
Where
r c
denotes the critical particle size associated with the critical velocity (
v r
), where the shifts from a particle-filled GB to a free-moving GB happens,
∫
r c
∞
n ‾
( r )
d r
the number of particles exceeding
r c
in size. As shown in Eq. (3), when the driving force for GB migration (
Δ F
) remains constant, the particle number and size determine the
v
. In this study, owing to the small concentration of CPDs, there are very limit CPDs particles/clusters located on GB region, thus particle size will be the dominant factor for
v
. Therefore, GB containing smaller size particles has significantly greater migration velocity than larger size particles. If the migration velocity of GB containing particles surpasses the critical velocity, the shifts from a particle-filled GB to a free-moving GB happens, and the particles cannot move with the GB anymore (Fig. 9(a2)). That is, the particles will undergo separation from the GB and subsequently migrate from the GB into the intragranular region. At the current thermomechanical consolidation conditions, the critical size of CPDs separated from GB is evaluated ∼60 nm, which makes CPDs clusters with sizes smaller than 60 nm tend to be distributed within the grain (Fig. 9(a3)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dislocation interaction behavior of different types of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: Micro-pillars compression and morphological analyses have indicated that CIL- and AIL-typed interface structures possess different dislocations-interface interactions, which significantly affect the composites deform ability. The deformation process of specimens containing CIL is shown schematically in Fig. 10 (a1-a3). For dislocations sliding along an arbitrary slip system, CIL will impede its further movement and lead to dislocation accumulation at the interface. Excessive dislocations plugging causes local stress concentration at the interface, and cracks will form due to the stress concentration, jeopardizing the materials ductility [52]. On the other hand, the Cu–C interface, as a typical “weak” interface, exhibits low interface shear strength. When dislocations in the Cu layers strike the crystal interface phase, the locally high stress levels will lead to lattice rotation within the Cu layers, which preferentially activates the slip system with higher Schmidt factor on this slip surface. As shown in Fig. 10(a3), preferential slip activation confined within a few Cu layers easily leads to deformation localization and hence shear instability [53]. Collectively, excessive dislocation plugging, low interfacial shear strength and lattice rotation combine to cause shear instability in samples containing CIL.

Conversely, for samples containing AIL, AIL can mediate inelastic slip/shear transfer simultaneously obviating stress concentration and preventing disastrous instability. The superior strain/shear accommodation capacity of AIL derives from the formation and transmission of shear transformation zones (STZs, clusters of Cu, O and C atoms that undergoing cooperative shear displacements) [54,55]. As shown in Fig. 10(b1), the STZs are generated when sliding dislocations on any priority-activated slip system strike the AIL. For an upcoming lattice dislocation, if STZs transformation is unsuccessful at one spatial location, it can still be done at another spatial point of the intersection line. This indicates that AIL has the capability to absorb and accommodate dislocations on arbitrary sliding systems as well as dissolve the accompanying geometric incompatibility [22]. Consequently, omnidirectional STZs could distribute the local strain arising from dislocation throughout the AIL. It would blunt the stress concentration related to the dislocations plugging and homogenize the plastic flow of composites. Subsequently, as the deformation continues, dislocations persistently activate other STZs near the interface and drive STZs to propagate into deeper amorphous regions (Fig. 10(b2)). When STZs strike the interface on the other side of AIL, a large amount of excess free volume will be introduced to the interface, which promotes the atomic reordering necessary for the dislocation emission process. Consequently, dislocations can be initiated or triggered on the other side of AIL and propagate within the Cu lattice to achieve dislocations cross-interface transmit (Fig. 10(b3)). Notably, the creation of STZs combined with the cross-interface transport of dislocations results in uniform deformation of the entire samples, instead of plastic deformation confined to a local Cu crystal layer. Moreover, both annihilation and multiplication of dislocations occur concurrently at AIL, resulting in a prolonged dynamic balance. This helps composites to achieve a longer lasting uniform strain while retaining high strength. Furthermore, from the perspective of inhibiting the generation of shear bands, the critical size (
L
A I L
) of AIL can be calculated by the aged-rejuvenation-glue-liquid model to evaluate the shear band formation (shown in EQ. (4)) [56]. When AIL is smaller than the critical size, the STZs inside the amorphous layer does not interconnect to form mature shear bands.
(4)
L
A I L
=
(
T g
−
T
e n v
)
2
∙
c v
2
∙ α
τ
g l u e
2
∙
c s
where
T g
denotes the glass transition temperature,
T
e n v
is the ambient temperature,
c v
is the volumetric specific heat,
α
is the metallic glass's thermal diffusivity,
c s
=
μ ρ
is the shear wave speed and
τ
g l u e
is the stress required to maintain the quasi-steady-state, which is usually a fixed value. By Eq. (4), it follows that
L
A I L
∼8 nm. TEM characterization (Fig. 4(c)) demonstrated that the AIL thick near CPDs is only 3–5 nm, which is less than the
L
A I L
, implying that no shear band will be generated inside the AIL. In conclusion, the excellent deformation stability of CPDs/Cu is attributed to the moderating effect of AIL on dislocations and its inhibition on shear bands.

In this study, ultrasonic spray pyrolysis was used to maximize the elimination of CPDs agglomeration, 80 % of the small-size CPDs particles/clusters (diameter less than 60 nm) are situated inside the grain interiors, displayed the typical intragranular distribution. Compared with the HT-CPDs, the high surface-active CPDs would promote the formation of amorphous interface layers (AIL). This stems from the fact that during SPS, activated O and suspended C atoms will heat escape from CPDs, and diffuse toward the interface to participate in the formation of Cu–O–C intermixing region, which ultimately forming the AIL at the interface. Due to the formation and transmission of shear transformation zones (STZs), the AIL-typed interface structures possessed higher strain accommodation ability during deformation, resisting the shearing/strain localization and deformation instability. Notably, the short process preparation method, as well as good combination of high strength, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity had offered novel strategy into the engineering of advanced structural-functional integrated Cu composites.

Zhaojie Li: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Liang Liu: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Rui Bao: Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Jianhong Yi: Validation, Supervision. Caiju Li: Methodology, Investigation. Yichun Liu: Validation, Supervision. Xin Kong: Validation, Supervision. Songlin Tan: Methodology, Investigation. Zunyan Xu: Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
